title: Mojaloop
subtitle: Fintech
intro: A ground-up rebuild of Mojaloop vNext, the open-source payment switch built for interoperable, high-volume transactions across banks, wallets, payment providers, and financial institutions.
description: ARG joined the core Mojaloop vNext team to help rebuild the open-source payment switch from the ground up. The previous implementation was no longer fit for the intended transaction flow, scale, and bank adoption path, so the work focused on redesigning the architecture around microservices, zero-trust service boundaries, controlled core dependencies, and compatibility with multiple ISO/payment-message standards.
challenge:
Mojaloop vNext needed to move beyond an implementation that was blocking the intended payment flow and limiting the path toward reliable bank adoption.

The goal was not to patch the old system, but to rebuild the open-source switch around clearer service boundaries, controlled core logic, and transaction flows that could support mass usage.

The platform also needed to interoperate with different financial institutions and payment-message standards, including ISO-based requirements, so banks and payment providers could integrate it without forcing every participant into the same internal model.
solution: Helped redesign Mojaloop vNext as a ground-up rebuild of the open-source payment switch
Reworked the platform around a microservice architecture with zero trust between services
Rebuilt core payment services with a controlled dependency surface so critical logic stayed understandable, maintainable, and owned by the project
Designed transaction flows for high-volume payment processing across banks, wallets, and payment providers
Supported compatibility with multiple ISO/payment-message standards to make bank adoption and integration more practical
Strengthened participant isolation, transaction orchestration, settlement flows, and operational reliability
Supported testing, QA, and migration work so the new version could move from architectural redesign toward production-grade adoption
impact: Helped move Mojaloop vNext toward a more controllable, secure, scalable, and bank-ready payment-switch foundation, capable of supporting high-volume transaction processing and integration across different financial institutions and payment-message standards.
stack: Express, MongoDB, Kafka, Angular, Ionic, Docker, Jest, Node

title: People's Clearinghouse
subtitle: Fintech & Financial Inclusion
problem: People's Clearinghouse is building a community-owned payments network that can move from pilots and summit demonstrations into production use with real banks, rural merchants, and social-sector financial institutions.
solutionBold: An ongoing Mojaloop vNext-based clearinghouse, mobile app, and merchant payment flow validated through a live Interledger Summit demo and national-scale switching performance tests.
description: The People's Clearinghouse extends the Mojaloop vNext work ARG has supported for years into a community-owned payment network for Mexican social finance, with the objective of connecting real banks and community financial institutions. At Interledger Summit 2025 in Mexico City, the client team demoed the platform live on the main stage; after the session, rural merchants used the app with summit attendees for a fictional-money market that exercised the full transfer flow. Behind the scenes, the payment APIs had already been load-tested at 1,000-2,000 requests per second with sub-second transfer processing and zero functional errors in multi-million request runs.
intro: A community-owned payments ecosystem for Mexico's social financial sector - extending Mojaloop vNext into a bank-ready clearinghouse, mobile app, merchant payment experience, and high-throughput payment API foundation.
challenge:
The People's Clearinghouse is an ongoing effort to bring community-owned payment infrastructure to real banks and social financial institutions. The Summit demo was one milestone in that path: a chance to show the platform moving value through real product surfaces while the broader objective remains production adoption by banks, community finance networks, and rural merchant ecosystems.

The underlying platform extended years of work with Mojaloop vNext and community banking rails into a PCH-specific ecosystem. That meant tying together clearinghouse flows, the mobile app, participant systems, QR merchant payments, admin confirmation screens, notifications, and the reliability needed for non-technical users.

The technical bar was national-scale. Payment API components needed to sustain 1,000-2,000 requests per second, keep lookup and quote workflows in the 200-300 ms range, maintain sub-second ledger transfer processing, and continue scaling linearly as workload increased.

The demo also had to survive the more revealing test after the presentation. Summit organizers brought merchants from rural communities and asked attendees to install the app and use fictional money to buy coffee, traditional clothing, and other goods that had already been covered by the event. Every purchase still had to travel through the full system.

The bar was practical: the experience needed to be understandable for first-time users, credible to the client and Summit audience, and reliable enough that roughly 100 live users could transact without transfer failures.
solution: Extended the Mojaloop vNext foundation into PCH-specific switch and participant flows for community banks, savings coops, and regulated payment institutions
Load-tested payment API components under sustained cluster conditions, processing roughly 1,000-2,000 requests per second while demonstrating linear scalability
Tuned lookup and quote workflows to average roughly 200-300 ms end to end, with ledger transfer flows averaging around 770 ms
Validated reliability through multi-million request test runs with zero functional errors across the exercised workflows
Built and refined mobile app paths for account transfers, QR merchant payments, confirmations, and transaction visibility across the full clearinghouse flow
Prepared the summit demo environment used by the client team for the main-stage presentation, including live transfer paths and dashboard confirmation screens
Supported the community showcase after the demo, where rural merchants sold coffee, traditional clothing, and other goods through the app using fictional money
Hardened the end-to-end request path so first-time summit users could install the app, transact, and see purchases clear without failed transfer requests
Kept the experience grounded in the client's long-running work with social finance, rural producers, real banks, and the Mojaloop vNext architecture being extended by the project
impact: At Interledger Summit 2025, People's Clearinghouse became a live public proof point: the client team demoed it on the main stage, rural merchants used it with attendees immediately afterward, and roughly 100 people moved fictional money through the full system without a single transfer request failing. The underlying APIs also proved they could support national-scale transaction switching patterns, sustaining 1,000-2,000 requests per second with sub-second transfer processing and zero functional errors during multi-million request tests.

metrics.label: Requests per second under sustained cluster load
Failed transfers during 100+ person live showcase

stack: Mojaloop vNext, Open Payments, Interledger Protocol, Event-Driven Architecture, Domain-Driven Design, Distributed Systems, Payment Switching, Ledger Orchestration, QR Payments, Merchant Payments, Mobile App, Core Banking

title: Dokutar
subtitle: Compliance Tech
intro: A compliance platform for German businesses that had outgrown its original Node and WordPress architecture and needed a scalable, secure, multitenant backend foundation.
description: ARG was brought in after Dokutar had outgrown its original Node and WordPress-based architecture. The platform needed to support more clients, stronger tenant isolation, and a scalable backend foundation, so we redesigned the backend and database, performed migrations, introduced multitenancy, and reinforced security to keep each client's data private and separated.
challenge:
Dokutar had grown beyond its original technical foundation. Parts of the system were built across Node and WordPress, which made scaling harder as the client base expanded and the product needed stronger data isolation between tenants.

The core problem was architectural: the backend, database model, and deployment approach needed to support more clients without creating data leakage risk, performance bottlenecks, or operational complexity.

Because the platform handled sensitive tax and process documentation, each client needed strict privacy guarantees. Multitenancy could not simply mean shared infrastructure; it required clear tenant separation, safer data access patterns, and stronger controls around sensitive information.
solution: Redesigned the backend architecture so the platform could scale beyond its original Node and WordPress-based foundation
Reworked the database model to support growth, safer data access, and tenant-aware workflows
Planned and executed migrations from the legacy setup into the redesigned backend foundation
Introduced multitenancy so multiple clients could operate on the platform with clear separation
Reinforced security controls to reduce the risk of client data exposure across tenants
Improved the technical foundation for compliance-heavy workflows, auditability, and future product growth
impact: Dokutar gained a backend foundation that could support growth without compromising tenant privacy. The platform became easier to scale, safer to operate, and better prepared for more clients, migrations, and compliance-heavy workflows.
stack: TypeORM, Express, React, Docker, Jest, MySQL, Node

title: Sky Tracks
subtitle: Music Tech
intro: A cloud music production platform rebuilt for a new product launch, with lower-latency performance, an Angular frontend migration, and support for external instrument connectivity.
description: ARG helped SkyTracks launch a new version of its browser-based music production platform. We migrated the frontend to Angular, implemented the updated design, reduced data latency, connected external instruments, and stabilized product features that had not worked reliably in earlier versions.
challenge:
SkyTracks needed to ship a new version of its cloud music production platform after earlier implementations left key features unreliable. The product had to support browser-based music creation while reducing latency enough for a smoother creative workflow.

The frontend also needed to move to Angular and match a new product design, without losing the complexity required by a music tool: instrument controls, audio interactions, project workflows, and responsive behavior across devices.

A major requirement was external instrument connectivity, so musicians could work with hardware inputs rather than being limited to in-browser controls.
solution: Migrated the frontend to Angular and implemented the redesigned product interface
Reduced data latency across interactive music workflows to make the platform feel more responsive
Connected external instruments so musicians could use hardware inputs inside the browser-based production environment
Stabilized features that were incomplete or unreliable in previous versions
Reworked product flows around music creation, editing, and playback so users could complete core tasks more consistently
Supported QA and release work needed to launch the new platform version
impact: SkyTracks was able to move toward a more reliable product launch with a modern Angular frontend, lower-latency interactions, external instrument support, and stabilized core features for browser-based music production.
stack: Knex, Tone.js, Tailwind, Koa, Angular, Docker, Jest, Node

title: TV Cine
subtitle: Entertainment
intro: A premium Portuguese entertainment platform where ARG helped expand product functionality across the user-facing experience, admin workflows, and backend API layer.
description: ARG helped TV Cine deliver new features across the public website and backoffice. The work included reshaping the Vue frontend, refactoring backend logic that had been constrained by the original Strapi setup, and adding a new API layer so both admins and users could access richer platform functionality.
challenge:
TV Cine needed to keep evolving its digital platform without being limited by the original implementation. New user-facing features had to fit the premium entertainment experience, while admins needed better backoffice tooling to manage content and platform operations.

The backend had also reached limits in its Strapi-based setup. To support the next set of features, the platform needed cleaner backend logic, a more flexible API layer, and frontend work in Vue that could carry the updated product experience.
solution: Implemented new user-facing product features across the TV Cine web experience
Reworked Vue frontend components and flows to support updated functionality
Refactored backend logic that had been constrained by the original Strapi-based implementation
Added a new API layer to expose richer functionality to both admins and users
Improved backoffice workflows so internal teams could manage platform content and operations more effectively
Supported QA and release work across frontend, backend, and admin-facing features
impact: TV Cine gained a more flexible platform foundation, with new public-facing features, improved admin workflows, and a backend/API structure better suited to ongoing product evolution.
stack: Vue.js, MongoDB, Entity Framework, .NET Core, Nuxt

title: Royalty Flush
subtitle: Music Rights
intro: A royalty-processing platform where ARG replaced spreadsheet-heavy workflows with a backend engine for CWR ingestion, royalty calculation, and reporting.
description: ARG automated a royalty-processing workflow that previously depended on Excel sheets and macros. We built a backend royalty engine for CWR file processing, royalty organization, obligation calculation, and reporting, giving the team a controlled, repeatable alternative to manual spreadsheet operations.
challenge:
Royalty Flush needed to replace a manual royalty workflow built around Excel sheets and macros. The process could handle some internal needs, but it was difficult to scale, audit, and operate reliably as royalty data grew.

The platform needed to ingest multiple CWR files, normalize royalty information, calculate obligations, and generate reports without relying on fragile spreadsheet logic.

The challenge was to turn a manual finance operation into backend software that could be repeated, reviewed, and trusted.
solution: Built a backend royalty engine to replace Excel-based processing
Implemented CWR file ingestion for multiple royalty input files
Structured royalty data so obligations could be calculated and reviewed consistently
Added reporting flows to make royalty outputs easier to inspect and share
Reduced dependence on manual macros by moving repeatable logic into backend services
Supported QA around calculation flows, file handling, and reporting output
impact: Royalty Flush moved royalty operations away from fragile spreadsheets and into a repeatable backend workflow, making CWR processing, royalty calculation, and reporting easier to operate and review.
stack: .NET Core, Entity Framework, Octopus Deploy, Docker, React, Hangfire, PostgreSQL, MediatR

title: Vector
subtitle: Fintech
intro: A crypto trading platform rebuilt from a non-functional prototype into a working product with authentication, exchange connectivity, strategy execution, and live trading flows.
description: ARG rebuilt Vector from a non-functional prototype into a working trading platform. The client arrived with an interface that looked complete but had no authentication, transaction flow, or live exchange activity, so we rebuilt the backend foundation, connected the frontend to real functionality, and implemented the systems needed for automated trading.
challenge:
Vector arrived as a prototype that looked complete on the surface but could not operate as a trading product. Authentication was missing, transaction flows were not functional, the frontend was not connected to real backend behavior, and exchange activity could not be executed.

The challenge was to rebuild the product foundation without discarding the intended user experience. The platform needed secure user access, exchange integrations, automated strategy execution, and live trading flows that could turn an illustrative interface into working financial software.
solution: Rebuilt the backend foundation around functional trading flows and secure user authentication
Connected the frontend to real backend behavior so the interface could move beyond static screens
Implemented exchange integrations for live trading activity
Built transaction flows for automated buy and sell execution based on strategy conditions
Added strategy-management functionality so users could configure or subscribe to trading logic
Supported QA around authentication, exchange behavior, trade execution, and dashboard state
impact: Vector moved from a non-functional prototype to a working trading platform with authentication, live exchange connectivity, automated transaction flows, and a frontend connected to real product behavior.
stack: MikroORM, Next.js, Kafka, PostgreSQL, Docker, Nx, Webpack, NestJS
